chunk_summary: Guidance on measles vaccination timing and routine immunization catch-up for children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM), emphasizing vaccination during discharge and follow-up care to prevent measles-related fatalities.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of measles vaccination in children with SAM and its timing during treatment and discharge.
Learn how to operationalize immunization checks in inpatient and outpatient workflows for SAM management.
Recognize the need for early measles vaccination in high-risk children and its implications for routine immunization schedules.
Differentiate between measles vaccination and vitamin A supplementation protocols in SAM care.
key_facts: Measles case fatality is higher in malnourished children, making vaccination critical.
Children with SAM who are ≥6 months and unvaccinated or incompletely vaccinated should receive a measles-containing vaccine unless in shock or acute instability.
Early measles vaccination (before 9 months) requires follow-up doses per the national schedule after 9 months to ensure durable immunity.
Immunization status should be reviewed at admission, discharge, and weekly outpatient visits during SAM care.
Missed routine immunization appointments should be treated as a follow-up failure risk similar to therapeutic feeding visits.
reference_quote: In standard hospital guidance, give a measles-containing vaccine to eligible children with SAM who are ≥6 months and are unvaccinated or incompletely vaccinated, but delay vaccination if the child is in shock or has uncontrolled acute instability. (WHO Pocket Book of Hospital Care for Children)
document_key_topics: Measles vaccination timing in SAM management
Routine immunization catch-up during SAM care
Operationalizing immunization checks in inpatient and outpatient workflows
Vitamin A supplementation protocols in SAM treatment
